title: 第 30 節
en: Lalita-vistara.

I have explained before why at present I attribute less importance than I did formerly to the occurrence of a number of titles, including Samkhya, Yoga, Vaiseshika, and possibly Nyaya, in the Lalita-vistara. If the date assigned by Stanislas Julien and others to certain Chinese translations of this work could be re-established, the passage so often quoted from the twelfth chapter would be of considerable value to us in forming an idea of Indian literature as it existed at the time when the Lalita-vistara was originally composed. We find here the names not only of the Vedic glossary (Nigha^^u ?) the Nigamas(part of JSTirukta), PuraT&as, Itihasas, Vedas, grammar, Nirukta, SiksM, jSTAandas, ritual (Kalpa), astronomy (Gyotisha), but, what would be most important for us, the names of three systems of philosophy also, S&mkhya, Yoga, and Vaiseshika, while Hetuvidya can hardly be meant for anything but Nyaya. But until the dates of the various Chinese translations of the Life of Buddha have been re-examined, we must abstain from using them for assigning any dates to their Sanskrit originals.
AOTaghosha's Buddha-^arita.

We may perhaps place more reliance on Asvaghosha's Buddha-^arita, which, with great probability, has been ascribed to the first century A. D. He mentions Vy&sa, the son of Sarasvati, as the compiler of the Veda, though not of the Vedanta-Sutras ; he knows Valmiki, the author of the Ram&yaTia, Atreya as a teacher of medicine, and <?anaka, the well-known king, as a teacher of Yoga. By far the most important passage in it for our present purpose is the conversation between Arac£a and the future Buddha, here 1 SJB.E,, vol. viii, p. 213.

already called Bodtdsattva in the twelfth book. This Arada is clearly a teacher of Samkhya-philosophy, it may be of Samkhya in an earlier state ; and, though the name of Samkhya does not occur, the name of Kapila does (XII, 21), and even a disciple of his is mentioned. Here then we have in a poem, ascribed to the first century A. D., a clear reference to that philosophical system which is known to us under the name of Samkhya, and we have actually the name of Kapila, the reputed author of that system. The name of Kapila- v&stu 1 also occurs, as the birthplace of Buddha and as the dwelling of the famous sage Kapila. No reference to the Ved&nta has been met with in Asva- f'losha's Buddha-&arita, though the substitution of the edantic name of Brahman for the Samkhya name of Purusha deserves attention.
BuddMst Suttas.

If we consult the Buddhist Suttas, which, whatever the date of their original composition may have been, were at all events reduced to writing in the first century B.C., and may be safely used therefore as historical evidence for that time, we find there also views ascribed to the Br&hmans of Buddha's time which clearly breathe the spirit of the Samkhya-philosophy. But it would be very unsafe to say more, and to maintain that such passages prove in any way the existence of fully developed systems of philosophy, or of anything very different from what we find already in certain Upanishads. All we can say is that there are a number of terms in the Suttas which are the very terms •used in the Ved&nta, Samkhya and Yoga-philosophies, such as Atman, $&svata, Nitya (? Anitya), Akshobhya, Brahman, l^vara, Dharma, Parrft&ma, and many more ; but, so far as I know, there is not one of which we could say that it could have been taken from the Sutras only, and from nowhere else.

We should remember that in the Buddhist Canon we find constant mention of Titthiyas or Tirthakas and their here- 1 I write Y^stu, because that alone means dwelling-place, while V&stu means thing. Vastu became Yatthu in Pali, and was then probably retranslated into Sanscrit as Vastu.

ASVALAYANAS Gtf/HYA-SUTRAS. 239 tical systems of philosophy. Six contemporaries of Buddha are mentioned, one of them, Niga?^Ao Nataputta, being the well-known founder of (rainism, PftraTCa Kassapa, Makkhali, Agrita, Pakudha and Sawgraya l. Nor are the names of the reputed authors of the six systems of Brahmanic-philosophy absent from the Tripifaka, But we hear nothing of any literary compositions ascribed to Badar&yarca, (raimini, Kapila, Patawgrali, Gotama or Kanida. Some of these names occur in the Buddhist Sanskrit texts also, such as the Lankavat&ra where the names of KaTi&da, Kapila, Akshapada, Brihaspati are met with, but again not a single specimen or extract from their compositions.
Asval&yana's Grikya-Sutras.

Another help for determining the existence of ancient Sutras and BMshyas may be found in the Gn'hya-Sutras of A&val&yana and $amkhayana, works belonging to the age of Vedic literature, though it may be to the very end of what I call the Sutra-period. Here, as I pointed out in 1859 in my History of Ancient Sanskrit Literature, we find not only the Rig-veda with all its subdivisions, but such names as Sumantu, (raimini, Vaisampayana, Paila, Sutras, Bhashyas, Bh&rata2, MaMbh&rata, teachers of the law, Cr&nanti, Bahavi, G&rgya, Gautama, Sakalya, B&bhravya, MS/ftcZavya, M&Tidukeya, G^rgi VataknavJ, Vadava Pr&titheyi, Sulabha Maitreyi, Kahola Kaushitaka, Mah^kaushitaka, Paimgya, Mahapaimgya, Suyagwa /Samkh^yana, Aitareya, Mahaitareya, the Sakala (text), the Bashkala (text), Sugfatavaktra, Audavahi, Mahaudavahi, Saugami, &unaka, Asvalayana. The ^amkh^yana Grihya-Siltras IV, 10, give the same list, though leaving out a few names and adding others. The most valuable part in both sets of Grihya-Sutras is their testifying at that early and probably pre-Buddhistic time, not only to the existence of Sutras, but of Bhashyas or commentaries also, without which, as I said before, neither the philosophical, nor the 1 Samawfo-Phala-Sutta 3.

2 How careful we must be, we may learn from the fact that instead of Bharata and Mahabharata, other MSS. read Bharatadharma&aryas ; while in the Samkhayana Gn'hya-Sutras IV, 10, 4, Bharata, Mnhabharata and Bharma7c§,ryas are left out altogether.
grammatical, nor any other Sfttras would ever have been intelligible, or even possible.
Did Buddha borrow from Kapila?

I may seem very sceptical in all this, but I cannot even now bring myself to believe that the author of Buddhism borrowed from the S&wkhya or any other definite system of philosophy, as known to us in its final Sutra form, in the sense which we ourselves assign to borrowing, Buddha, it seems to me, had as much right to many of the so-called Samkhya or Vedanta ideas as Kapila or anybody else. Who would say, for instance, that his belief in Samsara or migration of souls was borrowed from Badar&ya??a or Kapila? It belonged to everybody in India as much as a belief in Karman or the continuous working of deeds. In the great dearth of historical dates it may no doubt be excusable, if we lay hold of anything to save us from drowning while exploring the chronology of Indian literature. Our difficulties are very great, for even when the names of the principal systems of philosophy and the names of their reputed authors are mentioned, how do we know that they refer to anything written that we possess ? Unless we meet with verbatim quotations, we can never know whether a certain book of a certain author is intended, or simply the general Parampara, that is, the tradition, as handed down in various Asramas, two things which should be carefully distinguished.

It is strange to see how often our hopes have been roused and disappointed. We were told that in Professor Hardy's most valuable edition1 of the Anguttara a number of philosophical sects were mentioned which existed at the time of Buddha's appearance, such as (i) A^ivakas, (2) NigaTzi&as, (3) MuTic/as&vakas, (4) ffatfilakas, (5) Paribbagrakas, (6) M&gamKkas, (7) Teda?idikas, (8) Aviruddhakas, (9) Gotamakas, and (10) Devadhammikas. But not one of these names helps us to a real chronological date. A</ivakas and Niga?^&as are the names of (raina ascetics, the latter belonging to the Digambara sects, which could hardly have been established long before Buddha's appearance, while 1 The Pili Text Society, VoL iii, p. 276.

s, i.e. pupils o£ the shaveling, the Buddha, and Gotamakas would seem to be schools which owed their existence to Buddha himself. The other names Cra^ilakas, ascetics, ParibM^akas, religious mendicants, Tedaradikas, i.e. Samnyasins carrying the three staves, would be applicable both to Brahmanic and Buddhist sects. MagaWikas, if meant for Magadhikas, people of Magadha, would be Buddhists again. Aviruddhakas, a name not clear to me, may have been intended for ascetics no longer impeded by any desires, while Devadhamrnikas are clearly worshippers of the ancient national Devas, and therefore Brahmanic, and possibly Vedic. We get no historical Hates from the names of any of these schools, if schools they were. All they teach is that at the time Brahmanic and Buddhist sects were existing side by side in large numbers, but by no means, as is commonly supposed, in constant conflict with each other 1. Of the six recognised systems of philosophy, of their eponymous heroes or their written works, we do not hear a single word.
B&na's Harslia£arita.

Not even in later works, which have iJeen referred to the sixth, seventh, and eighth centuries A.D., do we meet with actual quotations from our Sutras of the six D arc anas. Bana, in his Life of King Harsha, knows indeed of Aupanishadas, K&pi]as, K&^adas; and if the K£pilas are the followers of the Samkhya, Ka-^adas the followers of the Vaiseshika school, the Aupanishadas can hardly be meant for anybody but the Vedantins. Varaha-Mihira also, in the sixth century A.D., mentions Kapila and Ka^abhuc/ (Vaiseshika), but even this does not help us to the dates of any Sutras composed by them.

The Chinese translator of the Karikas, likewise in the sixth century, informs us that these Karikas contain the words of Kapila or of Pa/liasikha, the pupil of Asuri, who was the pupil of Kapila. We are told even that there were originally 60,000 Gathas, and all that l^vara-Krishna did 1 Cf. Rkys Davids, J. R A. S., Jan., 1898, p. 197.. R 242 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.
was to select seventy of them for his seventy or seventytwo Karikas.

That Madhava (^350 A.D.), while mentioning the Sutras of the other systems, should not have mentioned those of the Samkhya, is no doubt, as I pointed out before, a strong argument in support of their non-existence in his time. But it is no proof, as little as we may conclude from the fact that Hiouen-thsang translated the Vaiseshika-nikayadasapadartha-sastra by ff/zana&andra, and not the Vaiseshika-Sutras by Kauada, that therefore these Sutras did not exist in his time. We cannot be too careful in such matters, for the unreserved acceptance of a purely conjectural date is very apt to interfere with the discovery of a real date. Hiouen-thsang likewise mentions a number of Ny&ya works, but not Gotama's Nyaya-Sfttras. Does that prove that Gotama's Sutras were unknown in the seventh century ? It may or may not. He relates that Gunamati defeated a famous S&mkhya philosopher of the name of M&dhava, but again he tells us no more. His own special study, as is wefi known, was the Yoga-philosophy. And here again, though he speaks of a number of Yoga works, he says not a word of the most important of them all, the Sutras of Patawgrali *. Yet I doubt whether we may conclude from this that these Sutras did not exist at his time.
The Tattva»sam&sa.

If then I venture to call the Tattva-samasa the oldest record that has reached us of the Samkhya-philosophy, and if I prefer to follow them in the account I give of that philosophy, I am quite aware that many scholars will object, and will prefer the description of the S&mkhya as given in the Karikas and in the Sutras. Both of them, particularly the Karikas, give us certainly better arranged accounts of that philosophy, as may be seen in the excellent editions and translations which we owe to Professor Garbe, and I may now add to Satish Chandra Banerji, 1898. If, as I believe, the Tattva-samasa-Sfttras are " older than our Sa/mkhya-Sutras, their account of the Samkhya-philosophy 1 M. M., India, p. 362.

THE TATTVA-SAMASA. 243 would always possess its peculiar interest from a historical point of view; while even if their priority with regard to the Karikas and Sutras be doubted, they would always retain their value as showing us in how great a variety the systems of philosophy really existed in so large a country as India.

These Samasa- Sutras, it is true, are hardly more than a table of contents, a mere Samkhyam or Pari-samkhy&, but that would only show once more that they presuppose the existence of a commentary from the very first. What we possess in the shape of commentaries may not be very old, for commentaries may come and go in different schools, while the Sutras which they intend to explain, would remain unchanged, engraved on the memory of teachers and pupils. How tenacious that philosophical Parampara was we can see from the pregnant fact that the Akhy&yikas or stories, though left out in the Karikas, must surely have existed both before and after the time of Isvara-Krislma, for though absent in the Tattva-samasa and in the K&rikas, they reappear in our Samkhya-Sutras. Where were they during the interval if not in Sfttras or Karikas, now lost to us?

The commentary on the Tattva-samasa, the publication of which we owe to Ballantyne, begins with an introduction which sounds, no doubt, like a late tradition, but reminds us in some respects of the dialogue at the beginning of the Chinese translation of the commentary on the Samkhyak&rik&s. But though it may sound like a late tradition, it would be very difficult to prove that it was so. Chronology is not a matter of taste that can be settled by mere impressions.

A certain Br&hman, we are told, overcome by the three kinds of pain, took refuge with the great J&shi Kapila, the teacher (not necessarily the originator) of the S&mkhya *, and having declared his family, his name, and his clan in order to become his pupil, he said : ' Reverend Sir, What is here on earth the highest (the bumnmm bonum)1 What is truth ? What must I do to be saved ? '
1 In the Bh&gavata-purana I, 3, n, ELapila Is said to have revived the S&mkhya (S&mkhya-Sara, ed. Hall, p. 7, note).
Kapila said, ' I shall tell thee.J Then follow the topics which are twenty-five in number: —
zh: 《廣遊經》(Lalita-vistara)。

我之前已經解釋過，為何我現在不如從前那樣重視《蓮花經》（Lalita-vistara）中出現的若干書名，包括《數論》（Samkhya）、《瑜伽》（Yoga）、《勝論》（Vaiseshika）以及可能的《因明論》（Nyaya）。倘若斯坦尼斯拉斯‧儒蓮（Stanislas Julien）及他人所指定的該著作某些漢文譯本的年代能夠重新確立，那麼從第十二章所頻繁引述的段落將對我們甚為寶貴，有助於我們形成對印度文學的認識——即《蓮花經》最初成書時期的印度文學狀況。我們在此處發現的不僅是吠陀詞彙書（Nighaṇṭu？）、《尼嘎麻》（Nigamas，《衍述》之一部分）、往世書、歷史書、吠陀、文法、《衍述》、音韻學（Śikṣā）、頌度（Chandas）、祭儀（Kalpa）、天文學（Jyotishā）等書名，更重要的是，我們還發現了三個哲學體系的書名，即《數論》、《瑜伽》及《勝論》，而《因論》（Hetuvidya）則幾乎無疑是指《因明論》。但在重新審查《佛陀傳》各種漢文譯本的年代之前，我們必須避免利用它們來為梵文原典賦予任何年代。
阿遮闍翁的《佛陀傳》(Aśvaghoṣa's Buddha-carita)

我們或許能更加信賴阿湿婆郭沙（Asvaghosha）的《佛本行經》（Buddha-carita），該著作很有可能著成於西元一世紀。他提及毗耶娑（Vyāsa），即莎羅斯瓦蒂之子，作為吠陀的編纂者，雖然並非《吠檀多經》（Vedanta-Sutras）的編纂者；他認識瓦爾米基（Valmiki），即《羅摩衍那》（Rāmāyana）的作者，以及阿特瑞亞（Atreya）作為醫學教師，和詹納卡（Janaka）這位著名的國王作為瑜伽教師。其中對我們當前目的最為重要的段落，莫過於阿羅剎（Arada）與未來佛之間的對話，¹ ¹ SJB.E., vol. viii, p. 213.

在第十二卷中已稱之為菩薩（Bodhisattva）。此阿剌陀（Arada）顯然是數論哲學（Samkhya）的教師，可能是早期數論哲學的教師；雖然數論之名未曾出現，但迦毘羅（Kapila）之名卻出現了（XII, 21），甚至還提及其弟子。因此，在一部歸屬於西元一世紀的詩作中，我們清楚地發現了對那個以數論之名而聞名的哲學體系的明確指涉，我們更實際見到了迦毘羅之名——即該體系公認的作者。迦毘羅城（Kapila-vastu）之名也出現過，作為佛陀的出生地，也作為著名賢人迦毘羅的住處。雖然用韋檀多派（Vedānta）關於梵我（Brahman）的名稱替代了數論派關於神我（Purusha）的名稱值得注意，但在馬鳴（Aśvaghośa）的《佛所行讚》（Buddha-carita）中尚未發現對韋檀多派的任何指涉。
佛教經典。

如果我們參閱佛教《經典》（Suttas），無論其原始編成的年代為何，這些典籍在公元前第一世紀已被寫成文字，因此可以安全地用作該時期的歷史證據。我們在其中同樣發現了許多觀點被歸於佛陀時代的婆羅門，這些觀點明確地呼應著數論哲學（Samkhya-philosophy）的精神。但要說得更進一步就非常危險了，維持那種主張——即這類段落以任何方式證明了完整發展的哲學體系的存在，或者證明了與我們已在某些《奧義書》中發現的情況大相逕庭的事物——這都不夠謹慎。我們只能說，《經典》中存在著許多術語，這些術語正是在《吠檀多》、數論與瑜伽哲學中使用的術語，例如阿特曼（Atman）、常恆（Śāsvata）、常住（Nitya / ? Anitya）、不搖動（Akshobhya）、梵（Brahman）、自在天（Īśvara）、法（Dharma）、演變（Parināma），以及許多其他術語。但就我所知，沒有一個術語可以說只能從《經文》（Sutras）中取得，而無法從其他任何地方取得。
我們應當記得，在佛教正典中，我們時常發現提提亞人（Titthiyas）或蒂爾塔卡人（Tirthakas）及其住所的提及。¹ ¹ 我寫作 Yāstu，因為只有這個字才是指「住所」之義，而 Vāstu 則指「物質」。Vāstu 在巴利語中成為 Yatthu，之後可能被重新譯回梵文為 Vastu。

阿濕瓦拉亞那《Gthf/HYA-SUTRAS》239 提及了與佛陀同時代的六位人物，其中一位是尼乾陀‧若提子（Nigantha Nataputta），他是耆那教的著名創立者。另外還有毗羅摩笈多（Parara Kassapa）、麻迦利‧瞿舍羅（Makkhali）、阿吉多‧翅舍鉤（Agrita）、樂波陀（Pakudha）和桑迦耶（Sawgraya）。在《三藏》中也出現了婆羅門六派哲學體系的據稱作者的名字。然而，我們對於任何歸屬於婆陀羅衍那（Badarayarca）、格拉依尼（Graimini）、卡皮拉（Kapila）、帕坦紗利（Patawgrali）、喬達摩（Gotama）或迦尼那（Kanida）的文學著作聞所未聞。其中某些名字也出現在佛教梵文典籍中，例如《楞伽經》（Lankavat‐ara），其中可見迦尼那（KaTi‐da）、卡皮拉（Kapila）、阿剎波陀（Akshapada）、婆利訶斯巴蒂（Brihaspati）等名字，但同樣地，我們未曾見到他們著作中的任何篇章或引文。
阿斯瓦拉亞那的《家庭經》(Asvalayana's Grikya-Sutras)。

另一項有助於確定古代蘇特拉（Sutra）與跋什耶（Bhashya）存在的幫助，可從阿什瓦拉亞那（Aśvalāyana）與商卡亞那（Śaṅkhāyana）的《家庭儀式蘇特拉》（Gṛhya-Sutras）中尋得。這些著作屬於吠陀文獻時代，儘管可能已至我所謂「蘇特拉時期」的末尾。正如我在1859年於《古代梵文文獻史》中所指出的，我們在此處發現不僅有梨俱吠陀及其所有分支，更有蘇曼圖（Sumantu）、迦伊尼（Caimini）、瓦依沙檬帕亞那（Vaiśampāyana）、派伊拉（Paila）、蘇特拉、跋什耶、婆羅多（Bhārata）、摩訶婆羅多（Mahābhārata）等名號，以及眾多法律教師的名字——克蘭提（Cānanti）、巴哈維（Bahavi）、歌爾吉亞（Gārgya）、高道瑪（Gautama）、薩卡利亞（Sakalya）、巴婆拉維亞（Bābhravya）、莫耳塔瓦亞（Mṛtcavya）、瑪圖杜克亞（Māṭdukeva）、歌爾吉‧瓦塔克瑙（Gārgi Vāṭaknavī）、瓦達瓦‧普拉蒂帖耶（Vadavā Prātitheyī）、蘇拉婆‧邁特雷耶（Sulabhā Maitreyī）、卡荷拉‧考希塔卡（Kahola Kauśitaka）、瑪哈‧考希塔卡（Mahākauśitaka）、派因吉亞（Paiṅgya）、瑪哈派因吉亞（Mahāpaiṅgya）、蘇亞吉瓦（Suyajvā）、商卡亞那（Śaṅkhāyana）、艾塔雷亞（Aitareya）、瑪哈艾塔雷亞（Mahāitareya）、薩卡拉本文（Sakala text）、巴什卡拉本文（Bashkala text）、蘇格那塔瓦克特拉（Sugṛtavaktra）、奧達瓦希（Audavahi）、瑪哈奧達瓦希（Mahaudavahi）、索伽密（Saugami）、舒那卡（Śunaka）、阿什瓦拉亞那（Aśvalāyana）等。商卡亞那《家庭儀式蘇特拉》第四卷第十章提供相同的名單，儘管省略了若干名字並補入其他名字。兩套《家庭儀式蘇特拉》中最有價值的部分，在於它們在這個早期且可能為前佛教時代，不僅見證了蘇特拉的存在，更見證了跋什耶或註疏的存在——沒有它們，如我之前所說，既無哲學性的，亦無
我們可以從以下事實了解到自己必須多麼謹慎：在某些寫本中，與其說是 Bharata 和 Mahabharata，不如說讀作 Bharatadharma­ryas；而在《三迦耶那家庭經》（Samkhayana Gṛhya-Sutras）第四卷第十章第四節中，Bharata、Mahabharata 和 Bharatacharyas 則被完全省略了。
文法上的，也不會有其他蘇特拉（Sutras）曾經被理解，甚或是可能存在。
佛陀是否借用了迦毗羅的思想？

我在這一切上似乎顯得很懷疑，但我到現在仍然無法說服自己相信佛教的創立者從數論派（Sāṃkhya）或任何其他確定的哲學體系借用了什麼——我們所知的那些最終的經文（Sutra）形式的體系——按照我們自己對「借用」的理解方式而言。在我看來，佛陀對所謂數論派或韋檀多派的思想有著和迦毗羅（Kapila）或任何其他人同樣多的權利。例如，誰會說他關於輪迴（Samsara）或靈魂遷移的信念是從波達羅衍那（Badarāyaṇa）或迦毗羅那裡借來的呢？在印度，它就像對業報（Karman）或業行持續作用的信念一樣，屬於每一個人。在歷史年代嚴重匱乏的情況下，如果我們抓住任何東西來拯救自己免於在探索印度文獻的年代學時溺水，這無疑是可以原諒的。我們的困難非常巨大，因為即使當提及主要哲學體系的名稱及其公認的創始人的名字時，我們如何知道它們指的是我們所擁有的任何書面著作呢？除非我們遇到逐字逐句的引用，否則我們永遠無法知道指的是某位作者的某部確定的書籍，還是僅僅是傳統（Paramparā），即那些在各種修行林（Āśrama）中代代相傳的傳統——這是應當仔細區別的兩件事。

令人驚異的是，我們的希望往往遭到喚起而後破滅。據說在哈代教授（Professor Hardy）極為寶貴的《增支部》（Anguttara）版本中1，提及了在佛陀出世時期存在的若干哲學教派，例如：（1）裸行者派（Aśvakas）、（2）尼乾陀派（Nigaṇṭhas）、（3）牟尼自在派（Muṇicāsvakas）、（4）裸體派（Nathalakas）、（5）遊行苦行派（Paribbajakas）、（6）大寂派（Magamikas）、（7）吠陀唯物派（Tedavidikas）、（8）非相違派（Aviruddhakas）、（9）喬達摩派（Gotamakas）以及（10）法天派（Devadhammikas）。然而，這些名稱中沒有一個能幫助我們確定真實的年代學日期。裸行者派（Aśvakas）和尼乾陀派（Nigaṇṭhas）是耆那教禁慾者的名稱，後者屬於天衣派（Digambara），這些派系似乎不太可能在佛陀出世之前許久就已建立；然而1《巴利文文獻學會》，第三卷，第二七六頁。

舍瓦林派（shaveling）的弟子、佛陀及喬達摩迦派似乎是源自佛陀本人的學派。其他名稱，如婆羅門弟子派（Craṇilakas）、苦行者、裸行派（Parivrājakas）、宗教乞士、特達羅迪迦派（Tedaradikas），即持三杖的桑雅新派（Samnyasins），既適用於婆羅門教派系，也適用於佛教教派。若馬迦瓦尼迦派（Magadhikas）指的是摩揭陀人，則又是佛教徒。阿毘盧陀迦派（Aviruddhakas），一個名義不甚清楚的派別，可能意指那些不再被任何欲望所阻礙的苦行者，而提婆達摩迦派（Devadharmmika）則明顯是古老民族提婆的崇拜者，因此屬婆羅門教派系，且可能涉及吠陀傳統。從這些學派的名稱中，假若它們確實是學派的話，我們無法獲得任何歷史性的事實。它們所揭示的僅是：在那個時代，婆羅門教派系與佛教派系並存於大量人口之中，但絕非如通常所認為的那樣，彼此處於持續的衝突狀態。關於六大公認的哲學體系、其同名創立人或其著述作品，我們聞所未聞。
毗那耶迦所作的《哈沙五世記》(B&na's Harshacarita)

即便在那些被認定為紀元後六、七、八世紀的後世著作中，我們也找不到關於六派正統哲學之經文（Sutras）的實際引用。婆那（Bana）在其《哈爾沙王傳》中確實提及奧義書派（Aupanishadas）、數論派（Kāpilas）和勝論派（Kāṇāda）；如果數論派（Kāpilas）是數論學派（Samkhya）的追隨者，勝論派（Kāṇāda）是勝論學派（Vaiseshika）的追隨者，那麼奧義書派（Aupanishadas）就不可能指代除了吠檀多派之外的任何人。六世紀紀元後的婆羅訶密希羅（Varaha-Mihira）也提及數論派（Kapila）和勝論派（Kāṇabhuc），但即使這樣也未能幫助我們確定由他們編纂的任何經文的年代。

同樣在六世紀的《迦里卡》（Karikas）中文譯者告訴我們，這些《迦里卡》包含了迦毘羅（Kapila）或帕利亞西卡（Pa/liasikha）的言論，帕利亞西卡是阿蘇里（Asuri）的弟子，而阿蘇里乃迦毘羅的弟子。我們甚至被告知原本有六萬首讚歌（Gathas），而伊濕伐羅－克里希那（I^vara-Krishna）所做的¹只是 ¹ 參見萊斯‧大衛斯（Rhys Davids），《英國亞洲學會期刊》（J. R. A. S.），1898年1月，第197頁。
是為了從中選出七十部吠陀經文，作為他的七十或七十二部頌詞（Karikas）之用。

馬德華（約西元350年）在提及其他派系的經文時，卻未曾提及數論派的經文，這無疑如我之前所指出的，強有力地支持了該經文在其時代不存在的論點。然而這並非證明，正如我們不能因為玄奘翻譯了優波舍叉（Upaśeṣa）與伊濕伐羅笈多（Īśvarakṛṣṇa）的《勝論經義論》（Vaiseshika-nikaya-dasapadartha-sastra）而非迦那陀（Kauada）的《勝論經》（Vaiseshika-Sutras），就據此斷定該經文在其時代並未存在一樣。在這類問題上，我們不能過於謹慎，因為對純粹推測性年代的毫無保留的接納，極容易妨礙發現真實年代。玄奘同樣提及許多尼耶亞（Nyāya）著作，卻未提及喬答摩（Gotama）的《尼耶亞經》（Nyaya-Sutras）。這是否證明了喬答摩的經文在七世紀不為人知？不一定。他記述了瑜伽行者古那摩蒂（Gunamati）擊敗一位名叫摩德華（Mādhava）的著名數論派哲學家，但他的敘述也僅止於此。如人所知，他自身的專門研究是瑜伽哲學。而在這方面，儘管他談及許多瑜伽著作，卻對其中最重要的帕坦伽利（Patañjali）的經文隻字未提。然而我對於我們能否由此斷定該經文在其時代不存在，仍表懷疑。
《諦義總略》(Tattva‧saMsa)

如果我冒昧地稱《諦諦瓦薩瑪薩》（Tattva-samasa）為傳世最古老的數論哲學（Samkhya-philosophy）記錄，並且在我對該哲學的敍述中選擇跟隨這部著作，我完全清楚許多學者將會反對，並傾向於採用《迦利迦》（Karikas）和《經》（Sutras）中所給出的數論哲學之敍述。這兩部著作，尤其是《迦利迦》，確實給我們提供了更加條理分明的該哲學之敍述，正如我們所欠於葛布（Garbe）教授的卓越版本與譯文所可見者；我現在更可加上薩蒂什‧錢德拉‧巴奈吉（Satish Chandra Banerji）1898年的成果。若如我所相信的，《諦諦瓦薩瑪薩經》（Tattva-samasa-Sutras）較我們的《數論經》（Samkhya-Sutras）為古老，則其對於數論哲學的敍述更值得重視。¹ ¹ M. M., *India*, p. 362.
《實在論綱》。243 從歷史觀點而言，它們始終具有特殊的價值；縱然有人質疑它們相對於《詩頌》（Karikas）和《經文》（Sutras）的優先地位，它們也永遠保有其價值，因為它們向我們展示了在像印度這樣廣袤的國度中，哲學體系的多樣性是何等豐富。

這些《數論經》（Samasa-Sutras）誠然不過是一份目錄表，單純的《數論》（Samkhyam）或《分類》（Pari-samkhya）而已，但這只會再次說明它們從一開始就預設了一份論釋的存在。我們所擁有的論釋形式也許並不十分古老，因為論釋可在不同學派中來來去去，而它們所欲詮釋的經文，卻會保持不變，銘刻於師徒的記憶中。那個哲學的傳承系統（Parampara）的堅韌性，我們可以從一個意蘊深長的事實看出：雖然《迦利迦》（Karikas）中遺漏了《故事集》（Akhyayikas），但這些故事必定在伊濕瓦羅‧克里希那（Isvara-Krishna）時代的前後都存在著，因為雖然《本質攝論》（Tattva-samasa）和《迦利迦》中沒有它們，但它們卻在我們的《數論經》中重新出現。那麼它們在這個間隔期間在哪裡呢？如果不在如今已失佚的《經》或《迦利迦》中，還能在何處？
《本質論綱》(Tattva-samasa) 的註釋書是由巴蘭泰尼 (Ballantyne) 出版的，其開篇有一段導言，雖然無疑聽起來像是晚期的傳統，但在某些方面卻讓我們想起《數論頌》(Samkhyakārikā) 的註釋書中文譯本開篇的對話。然而，儘管它聽起來像是晚期的傳統，但要證明它確實如此將會極其困難。年代學並非一個可以由單純的印象來決定的品味問題。
一位婆羅門，據記載，被三種痛苦所克服，遂投靠偉大的仙人迦毘羅（Kapila），他是數論派（Sāṃkhya）*的教師（不必然是創始者），在宣告了他的家族、名字和種姓以便成為其弟子後，他說：「尊敬的先生，世間最高的善（the summum bonum）是什麼？什麼是真理？我必須做什麼才能得救？」
在《薄伽梵往世書》第一冊第三章，據說伊拉皮拉（Elapila）曾復興過數論派（參見《數論要義》（Sāṃkhya-Sara），Hall版，第七頁注釋）。
迦毗羅說：「我將告訴你。」隨後是二十五個主題：——
